retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Chronic Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: If the oral microbes and their by-products remain after the initial host response, the lesion may turn from a neutrophil-dominated lesion to a macrophage-dominated lesion encapsulated in connective tissue. [7] Unlike their acute counterpart, chronic lesions are often asymptomatic. Due to the destruction of the periodontal ligament and the alveolar bone, radiographic changes become evident.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Advanced lesion: The advanced lesion signifies the progression from gingivitis to periodontitis. Bacterial biofilm extends into the periodontal pockets, creating an optimal environment for anaerobic bacteria to proliferate. At this stage, irreversible loss of attachment of the junctional epithelium and alveolar bone loss occur. The loss of gingival fibers and alveolar bone defines this stage, which can vary based on microbial and host factors. [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Biopsies -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Hard Tissue: Lesions
retrieved_docs.content: Bony lesions of the maxilla and mandible are characterized by their radiographic appearance. Many of these lesions arise from cells of odontogenic origin or cell lineages associated with tooth development, as teeth are the main structures within the gnathic complex. On imaging, bony lesions can be radiopaque (hyperdense), radiolucent (hypodense), or present with a mixed picture. Radiopaque lesions are common and often represent a subset of conditions that do not require biopsy. Single areas of asymptomatic hyperdensity with no destruction or expansion are often found, and the differential diagnosis includes idiopathic osteosclerosis, cemento-osseous dysplasia, and condensing osteitis, usually from a tooth cavity that creates a low-grade inflammatory stimulus over time.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: As the invading oral microflora and the immune response clash, the periapical tissues surrounding the tooth's root, such as the periodontal ligament and alveolar bone, can be destroyed. This destruction causes periodontal lesions, which may vary in clinical manifestations. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal disease refers to a group of conditions that affect the periodontium, the supporting structures of the teeth, including the gingiva, alveolar bone, cementum, and periodontal ligament. Early detection and timely management are essential to prevent long-term complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: As compared to chronic periodontitis, aggressive periodontitis has a marked group of characteristics that defines the disease very well making it simple to diagnose it. Baer et al. 1971, defined aggressive periodontitis as "a disease of the periodontium occurring in otherwise healthy adolescent, which is characterized by a rapid loss of alveolar bone around more than one tooth of the permanent dentition." [31] The presence of local factors like calculus is not commensurate with the amount of periodontal tissue destruction. [31] The classical case presentation of aggressive periodontitis includes early age onset of disease, vertical bone loss in molars and incisor teeth leading to mobility, familial aggregation, rapid progression of the disease. [14] Aggressive periodontitis subclassifies into localized aggressive periodontitis (LAP) and generalized aggressive periodontitis (GAP). LAP is characterized by bone loss including permanent molars and incisors while GAP involved most of the permanent teeth. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Page and Schroeder were the first to describe the histopathology of periodontal disease, identifying 4 distinct stages based on clinical and histological tissue changes. These stages—initial, early, established, and advanced—illustrate the progressive nature of the disease, beginning with subclinical inflammation and potentially leading to irreversible tissue destruction and bone loss. Initial lesion: The initial lesion is characterized by plaque accumulation, which leads to vascular changes and intercellular gap formation, thereby increasing the flow of gingival crevicular fluid. Adhesion molecules attract polymorphonuclear neutrophils (PMNs) to the lesion, while T lymphocytes modulate fibroblast activity in the affected area. Clinically, this stage is asymptomatic.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis is based on comparing clinical findings with a healthy periodontium, which is characterized by stippled, pale pink gingiva, a 1 to 3 mm sulcus, and no bleeding (see Image. Healthy Periodontium). Signs of periodontal disease include bleeding, pain, bad taste or odor, pocketing, radiographic bone loss, and tooth mobility. Deep periodontal pockets suggest active microbial-host interactions. Without intervention, progressive bone loss can result in increased tooth mobility and eventual tooth loss. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Gingivitis is the initial stage of the response of the body towards local factors present in the oral cavity; this is a reversible process without the loss of any bone or periodontal support. [11] [12] Histopathologically, within the lamina propria collagen fibers are destroyed and leading to ulcerations in the sulcular epithelium. [26] According to Page et al., there are three histopathological stages of gingivitis, i.e., initial lesion, early lesion, and established lesion. [26] These lesions are marked by different cells demarcating the transition between these stages. Further inflammatory changes in the gingiva lead to the establishment of periodontal disease by the transition of the established stage to the advanced stage. [26] The spread of inflammation from epithelium to connective tissue takes place laterally and apically resulting in the destruction of collagen fibers. This destruction of collagen fibers presents clinically as "attachment loss" marking the shift from gingivitis to periodontitis. Gradually due to the activation of osteoclast cells, bone resorption is initiated leading to gradual tooth loss. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A periapical radiolucency is usually an indication of an endodontic abscess or a combination of a periodontal and endodontic abscess. The latter often demonstrates characteristic alveolar bone loss extending to the periapical lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Pathophysiology -- Periodontitis and Necrotizing Periodontal Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontitis is an infection of the pocket surrounding the base of the tooth and gum tissue. It can almost universally be attributed to untreated dental caries spreading to the gingival epithelium. Advanced periodontitis can lead to irreversible loss of tooth attachment and alveolar bone loss. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal abscesses, which are localized purulent infections found in the periodontium surrounding the tooth. [43] [44] [45] Endodontic lesions, which are localized infections originating from the tooth pulp. These infections can spread through the root tissue into the surrounding periodontium, potentially mimicking periodontal disease. [43] [44] [45] Leukemia may present as gingival enlargement accompanied by bleeding gums, often mimicking periodontal diseases such as gingivitis. [46] Certain medications, including calcium channel blockers, immunosuppressants, and anticonvulsants, can cause gingival hyperplasia, which may clinically resemble gingivitis. [47] [48] Squamous cell carcinoma can cause extensive bone loss and mimic periodontal disease that is refractory to mechanical and pharmacological treatments. [49] [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Necrotizing Periodontal Diseases -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic findings range from minimal to extensive changes. Osteonecrosis may appear as a moth-eaten, patchy radiolucency, similar to features seen in medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw, osteoradionecrosis, or osteomyelitis. Radiopaque sequestra may also be present. Computed tomography offers greater detail and aids in distinguishing among osseous pathologies. A definitive diagnosis depends on clinical findings and patient history.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal disease may depict as gingival or periodontal abscess, acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis, endodontic-periodontal lesions. [37] In cases of localized periodontal disease, the origin of the disease requires differentiation between pulpal and periodontal origin. Non-responding cases of periodontitis categorize as refractory periodontitis. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Periapical radiographs are critical in evaluating the periodontal hard tissues. Widening of the periodontal ligaments and horizontal or vertical bone loss is often expected.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A periodontal abscess is described as a localized accumulation of pus within the gingival wall of a periodontal pocket. More prevalent in patients with previous periodontal pockets, [1] it develops rapidly, destroying periodontal tissues and depicting clear symptoms. If the tooth is associated with multiple abscesses, it may have a “hopeless prognosis.” [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2655
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of bone cancer. The identification of a malignant cellular infiltrate reflects the invasion of cancerous cells into bone tissue, while a secondary lesion denotes additional disease sites that arise as the primary bone tumor progresses. Recognizing these findings together aids clinicians in confirming the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم, assessing disease extent, and guiding appropriate therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2564
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that this malignant condition involves the upper jaw and reflects a neoplastic process affecting both bone and connective tissue structures in that region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is identified as a pathological finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of infiltrating malignant cells within the affected bone tissue. Likewise, Tumor cells, malignant are recognized as the abnormal cellular component that characterizes سرطانة العظم, reflecting the disease’s neoplastic nature. Together, these findings underscore that both the infiltrative pattern of malignant cells and the presence of malignant tumor cells are key diagnostic features of سرطانة العظم, aiding clinicians in confirming the diagnosis and guiding appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2806
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is identified as a diagnostic finding associated with the disease سرطانة العظم, indicating that secondary tumor deposits have occurred within bone tissue. The presence of a metastatic lesion in a patient suspected of having سرطانة العظم suggests disease spread beyond the primary site, which has important implications for staging, prognosis, and therapeutic planning. Recognizing metastatic lesions through imaging or pathology therefore aids clinicians in assessing the extent of سرطانة العظم, guiding decisions about systemic therapy, surgical intervention, and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2168
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary Lesion is identified as a finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the presence of secondary lesions may support the diagnosis of this bone malignancy. Conversely, Tumor cells, benign are not considered a finding of سرطانة العظم, meaning that benign tumor cells do not contribute to the identification or confirmation of this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2232
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). In the clinical evaluation of patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of secondary lesions indicates disease spread or metastatic involvement, which is essential for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and formulation of appropriate therapeutic strategies. Recognizing secondary lesions therefore plays a critical role in the comprehensive management of bone cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2369
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are recognized as diagnostic findings associated with the disease سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). In contrast, the presence of benign tumor cells does not constitute a finding of سرطانة العظم, indicating that benign cellular morphology helps exclude this malignant bone condition. Consequently, identification of metastatic or secondary lesions supports the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم, whereas benign tumor cells suggest a non‑malignant process.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2711
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate, Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are all recognized as clinical findings of both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes and سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). In the context of Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes, these findings indicate that the disease often presents as a secondary lesion in the supraclavicular region and is characterized by the presence of malignant tumor cells. Likewise, in سرطانة العظم, Malignant Cellular Infiltrate, Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion serve as diagnostic indicators, and Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component of the disease. Together, these relationships underscore that bone cancer can give rise to metastatic malignant neoplasms in supraclavicular lymph nodes, with overlapping pathological features such as malignant cellular infiltrates, metastatic lesions, secondary lesions, and malignant tumor cells, all of which are important for accurate diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2513
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Metastatic Lesion are both recognized as pathological findings of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The presence of a malignant cellular infiltrate indicates that cancerous cells have invaded the bone tissue, while a metastatic lesion reflects the spread of tumor cells to bone from another primary site. Identifying either of these findings on imaging or histopathology supports the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم and guides clinicians in staging the disease, planning appropriate treatment, and monitoring for disease progression.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1896
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate, Metastatic Lesion, and Secondary Lesion are all recognized as disease‑related findings that point to a Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes. This metastatic neoplasm to the supraclavicular nodes is itself classified as a Secondary Lesion and is identified by the presence of Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Metastatic Lesion, while benign tumor cells (Tumor cells, benign) are explicitly noted as not being present. In the context of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer), the same three entities—Malignant Cellular Infiltrate, Metastatic Lesion, and Secondary Lesion—are each considered positive disease findings, reinforcing their role as indicators of metastatic bone cancer involvement. Conversely, benign tumor cells are consistently reported as absent in both the supraclavicular metastatic disease and in سرطانة العظم, underscoring that benign cellular components are not expected in these malignant processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.
